title: Teaching & Outreach
text: I care about creating welcoming spaces where people can explore geoscience, develop technical skills, and communicate ideas with confidence.

title: Teaching & Outreach
text:
Teaching and mentoring
My teaching emphasizes curiosity, clear communication, practical problem-solving, and an inclusive learning environment.





University teaching
Courses and teaching assistant roles in geology, probability and statistics, physical chemistry, geologic hazards, and field-based learning.







Technical training
Python training, coding workshops, data visualization, and practical programming for geoscience.

title: Teaching & Outreach
text:
Outreach and science communication
I enjoy making geoscience accessible through public engagement, bilingual communication, and creative approaches—including lessons learned through improvisational theater.

title: Teaching & Outreach
text:
Mentoring and field learning





Mentoring is part of how I think about science: helping students build confidence with field observations, quantitative tools, and the process of asking research questions.

title: About
text:
I am a tectonic geomorphologist from Santiago, Chile. I study how tectonics, climate, erosion, and sediment transport shape landscapes across timescales, from active faults and river networks to long-term exhumation and mountain evolution.
My work combines field geology, remote sensing, geochronology, thermochronology, numerical modeling, and scientific programming. I am especially interested in places where the landscape preserves a complicated record: faults that slip slowly, deserts where water is rare but powerful, sediment that can hide or amplify tectonic signals, and mountains that carry the memory of climate change.
I moved to the United States in 2020 to pursue graduate work in Earth and Space Sciences at the University of Washington. Before that, I studied geology at the University of Chile and worked on field campaigns across Chile, including projects related to active tectonics, natural hazards, Quaternary mapping, and engineering geology.
I also care deeply about inclusive geoscience, teaching, mentoring, outreach, and science communication. I am part of communities that support Latin American and Latinx geoscientists, and I value bilingual science communication and technical training.
Outside the usual academic box, improvisational theater has shaped how I think about communication: listening carefully, responding clearly, and making space for other people to contribute.





In the field
Fieldwork is where many of my questions start: reading geomorphic markers, collecting samples, and connecting landscape form to process.

title: Tools & Code
text: My technical work connects field questions with quantitative workflows: satellite data, topography, geochronologic constraints, landscape-evolution models, and clear scientific visualization.

title: Tools & Code
text:
Numerical modeling
Landlab and landscape-evolution modeling for testing how climate variability, sediment cover, and fault slip influence river networks and fault-zone morphology.

title: Tools & Code
text:
Remote sensing
Sentinel-1 InSAR, MintPy, GNSS integration, GIS, DEM analysis, geomorphic markers, and topographic metrics.

title: Tools & Code
text:
Geochronology and thermochronology
AHe, ZHe, AFT, HeFTy modeling, luminescence sampling, cooling histories, and integration with geologic constraints.

title: Tools & Code
text:
Programming and visualization
Python, MATLAB, PyGMT, TopoToolbox, reproducible notebooks, data visualization, and figure design.

Add later: selected public repositories, example notebooks, workshop materials, and links to code that is cleaned enough to share.

title: Research
text: My research asks how landscapes record interacting tectonic and climatic processes. I use field observations, remote sensing, low-temperature thermochronology, geochronology, and landscape-evolution modeling to connect surface processes with fault slip, sediment transport, erosion, and mountain building.

title: Research
text:
Field and analytical work





Sampling landscape memory
Sediment samples, geomorphic markers, and remote observations help connect the field record to the timing and mechanics of landscape change.

title: Tamara Aránguiz-Rago
text:
I am a tectonic geomorphologist interested in how faults, climate, erosion, and sediment transport shape Earth’s surface.
My research combines field observations, remote sensing, geochronology, thermochronology, and numerical modeling to understand how landscapes respond to tectonic and climatic forcing.
Research Publications

title: Tamara Aránguiz-Rago
text:
I am a tectonic geomorphologist interested in how faults, climate, erosion, and sediment transport shape Earth’s surface.
My research combines field observations, remote sensing, geochronology, thermochronology, and numerical modeling to understand how landscapes respond to tectonic and climatic forcing.
Research Publications

title: Tamara Aránguiz-Rago
text:
Research themes





Active faults and landscape response
Slowly slipping faults leave measurable records in topography, drainage networks, sediment deposits, and surface deformation.







Climate, sediment, and landscape evolution
Numerical models help test how climate variability and sediment cover influence river dynamics, divide migration, and fault-zone morphology.







Long-term exhumation and mountain evolution
Thermochronology and geologic constraints reveal cooling, exhumation, basin development, and landscape evolution over millions of years.

title: Tamara Aránguiz-Rago
text:
Methods


Field geology and geomorphic mapping
Satellite remote sensing, InSAR, GNSS, and topographic analysis
Geochronology, thermochronology, and sediment records
Landscape-evolution modeling and scientific programming







Fieldwork, samples, and models
The work moves between landscapes, samples, satellite data, and code: collecting sediment records in the field, measuring surface deformation and topography, and testing process-based explanations with numerical models.
